next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 13

By the first week of November, Nate had turned avoiding Lazar's Hardware into something with the complexity of a bad software workaround. He could eat at Gail's without looking toward the end unit. He could wave if Mort happened to be out front sweeping leaves off the sidewalk. He could stand at the counter in the diner and listen to Gail complain about the city putting up another temporary sign for the future development and keep his eyes on his coffee until Mort's voice passed behind him and went away again. There was a whole stable operating system for this now: perimeter only, no entry, no touching anything he remembered.

It failed on a Saturday because there were three dead tulip bulbs on the windowsill over the kitchen sink and because Sarah had said, without looking up from the stack of papers she was grading, "If you're going out, can you pick up a level? The shelf in the bedroom is still crooked."

She had said it in the same tone she might have used to ask for milk. Nothing had been resolved after the fight two weeks earlier. She had moved back into the bed three nights ago, but not all the way back into him. They talked. They ate dinner. She handed him things and asked for things and sometimes her face softened when she forgot to be wary, but the apartment still had the feeling of a room after somebody threw a glass in it. It was not broken exactly. It had just changed. He had said sure because it was a shelf and a level and a thing a boyfriend could buy on a Saturday without screwing it up.

Then he stood in the strip mall parking lot with the eng...
open_hooks_to_carry: The trigger: a small domestic failure (wrong drawer) that opens onto months of accumulated evidence
Sarah's catalog of anomalies — precise, specific, devastating in their accuracy
prev_tail_excerpt:
...ail and never once say the word destroy.

Nate had built his adult life around a story because the story held the pieces in place. Mort had died because Dominic Altieri killed the store. Nate had killed Dominic Altieri because some acts deserved an answer. The story was ugly but load-bearing. Without it the weight did not go away. It just dropped lower, onto the thing itself. A 34-year-old man in 2025 had aimed a machine at a 31-year-old stranger in 2005 and erased him. Then he had gone on wearing the stranger's face while taking the stranger's job, the stranger's family, the stranger's bed, and telling himself there had at least been a reason.

There had been a letter.

There had been a signature.

There had also been this. The months of trying. The names. The spreadsheets. The stupid hope of a person who thought if he explained the damage clearly enough, somebody above him might decide not to do it.

Nate turned back to the monitor and read the March message one last time, slowly enough to hear the cadence of the sentences in his head without pretending it was Dominic's voice exactly. It was just a man's written voice, careful and competent, tired by the end but not cynical yet.

He had typed Morton's name because he was trying to keep Mort from getting erased by the process.

Nate sat very still in Dominic's chair, his hand on Dominic's mouse, Dominic's inbox at his back and Dominic's sent mail open in front of him, and understood with a flat, nauseating clarity that would not improve no matter how many times he read it again:

He had killed a man who was trying to help.
state_deltas_required: Sarah's accumulated observations about the changes in Dominic — months of wrong notes, behavioral shifts, and emotional distance — reaching the point where silence is no longer possible

The relationship reaches crisis. Sarah cannot identify the specific truth but she has diagnosed the damage correctly: the man she's living with is fundamentally absent. Nate's deception is exposed as a form of emotional violence even without the literal secret being revealed.
